section: Chapter 10: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

questions.question: What is the function of the human eye?
questions.correct_answer: It helps us see objects by detecting light and forming images on the retina.
questions.explanation: The human eye acts like a camera, focusing light onto the retina to produce images.

questions.question: What is the retina?
questions.correct_answer: The light-sensitive screen inside the eye where the image is formed.
questions.explanation: The retina contains photoreceptor cells that detect light and send signals to the brain.

questions.question: What is the function of the eye lens?
questions.correct_answer: It focuses light onto the retina to form a clear image.
questions.explanation: The eye lens can change its shape to adjust focus for near and distant objects.

questions.question: What is accommodation of the eye?
questions.correct_answer: The ability of the eye lens to change its focal length to focus on objects at different distances.
questions.explanation: Ciliary muscles adjust the shape of the lens to focus images clearly on the retina.

questions.question: What is the least distance of distinct vision for a normal human eye?
questions.explanation: Objects closer than this distance appear blurred for a normal eye.

questions.question: What is persistence of vision?
questions.correct_answer: The ability of the eye to retain an image on the retina for about 1/16 of a second.
questions.explanation: This phenomenon allows us to perceive motion in movies and animations.

questions.question: What is scattering of light?
questions.correct_answer: The phenomenon in which light is redirected in different directions when it passes through a medium.
questions.explanation: Scattering occurs due to interaction of light with particles in the atmosphere.

questions.question: Why is the sky blue?
questions.correct_answer: Because shorter wavelengths of light (blue) are scattered more by the atmosphere.
questions.explanation: This phenomenon is known as Rayleigh scattering.

questions.question: Why does the sun appear reddish during sunrise and sunset?
questions.correct_answer: Because most of the shorter wavelengths are scattered, leaving red light to reach our eyes.
questions.explanation: At sunrise and sunset sunlight travels a longer path through the atmosphere.

questions.question: What is the Tyndall effect?
questions.correct_answer: The scattering of light by small particles in a medium.
questions.explanation: It makes the path of light visible in fog, smoke, or dusty air.

notes.title: Human Eye
notes.content: An optical instrument that helps us see objects by forming images on the retina.

notes.title: Accommodation
notes.content: The process by which the eye lens changes shape to focus on near or distant objects.

notes.title: Persistence of Vision
notes.content: Retention of an image on the retina for about 1/16 second after the object is removed.

notes.title: Scattering of Light
notes.content: The phenomenon in which light is redirected when it interacts with particles in the atmosphere.

notes.title: Tyndall Effect
notes.content: The scattering of light by small particles that makes the path of light visible.

facts.fact: The least distance of distinct vision for a normal human eye is about 25 cm.
The retina contains light-sensitive cells that send signals to the brain.
Blue light is scattered more than other colours because it has a shorter wavelength.
The sky appears blue due to scattering of sunlight by the atmosphere.
The reddish colour of the sun at sunrise and sunset occurs due to longer atmospheric path length.
